title: What is 3D Object Detection?
concept: 3D object detection predicts oriented 3D cuboids that capture depth, size, and rotation in physical space.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main innovation of DETR compared to CNN-based detectors?

options.text: Using a Transformer encoder-decoder to predict bounding boxes directly as a set
Using default anchor boxes on multiple layers
Running NMS on raw pixel coordinates

exercises.explain: Correct! DETR uses a Transformer to predict a direct set of boxes, removing anchors and NMS.

cards.title: The Lost Dimension
cards.body: A 2D bounding box tells you where an object is on your flat screen, but it doesn't tell you how far away it is or if it's tilted. If a self-driving car only saw in 2D, it would have no idea how close a pedestrian is! How do we see in 3D?

cards.title: What is 3D Object Detection?
cards.body: 3D Object Detection predicts 3D oriented bounding boxes (often called cuboids) around objects. Unlike 2D boxes, a 3D box captures the object's depth (distance from the sensor) and its exact 3D rotation in physical space.

cards.title: Flat Card vs. Cardboard Box
cards.body: 2D detection is like placing a flat playing card over an object on a table. 3D detection is like building a cardboard box that you can pick up, rotate, and place exactly over the object, matching its length, width, and height in the real room.

cards.title: Robot Gripper
cards.body: A robot arm sees a box on a shelf. A 2D detector only gives pixel coordinates on screen. A 3D detector calculates that the box center is exactly 1.2 meters away, 0.4 meters right, and rotated at a 30-degree angle, allowing the robot to grab it without knocking it over.

cards.title: Just a 2D Box with Depth?
cards.body: You might think 3D detection is just drawing a 2D box and guessing distance. Fact: 3D detection is a multi-dimensional regression problem that models physical spatial geometry (rotation, length, width, height, center) and is far more complex than simple 2D scaling.

left.text:
4 variables: [x, y, width, height]
Flat rectangular overlay
No depth or rotation details

right.text:
9 variables: [x, y, z, w, h, l, roll, pitch, yaw]
3D oriented cuboid box
Captures distance and physical pose

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between 2D and 3D object detection?

options.text: 3D detection predicts depth and oriented rotation in space, while 2D detection only predicts flat screen coordinates
2D detection only works on videos, while 3D works on photos
3D detection does not use neural networks

exercises.explain: Correct! 3D detection predicts 3D cuboids that capture depth and physical orientation.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A 3D oriented bounding box is also known as a cuboid.
exercises.explain: True. 3D boxes have length, width, and height, forming a 3D cuboid volume.

exercises.sentence: Unlike flat 2D rectangles, 3D bounding boxes capture ___ (distance from the camera) and rotation in space.
exercises.explain: Depth (or distance) is the third dimension added by 3D object detectors.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following applications strictly requires 3D object detection?

options.text: A self-driving car planning a safe path around a parked vehicle
A web search engine categorizing dog photos
A facial recognition filter that adds sunglasses to an image

exercises.explain: Correct! A self-driving car must know the physical distance and orientation of other vehicles to plan safe steering.

exercises.prompt: A warehouse drone needs to land on a landing pad. Why is a 2D detector insufficient for this task?

options.text: A 2D detector cannot calculate the drone's height above the pad or the tilt angle of the pad, which is critical for landing safely.

exercises.explain: Exactly! 3D spatial properties (height, distance, tilt) are mandatory for physical interactions like drone landing.
